PROMPT: I'm planning a trip to visit national parks in Utah and need to compare two specific parks: Zion National Park and Bryce Canyon National Park. First, search for all national parks in Utah (state code 'UT') and from the full list, find and note the park codes for both 'Zion National Park' and 'Bryce Canyon National Park'. Then get the detailed information for Zion National Park using its park code, and from that detailed response (which contains many fields), extract and remember Zion's exact latitude and longitude coordinates. Next, get the detailed information for Bryce Canyon National Park using its park code, and from that detailed response, extract and remember Bryce Canyon's exact latitude and longitude coordinates. Then get the current alerts for both parks (using both park codes together, comma-separated). Finally, use the coordinates you extracted for both parks to calculate the driving distance and time between Zion National Park and Bryce Canyon National Park. Report: (1) the driving distance in km/miles, (2) the estimated driving time, and (3) how many total alerts are currently active across both parks.
GTFA_CLAIMS: [{"step": 1, "description": "Call national-parks_findParks for Utah (stateCode='UT'), returns a large list of all Utah national parks with many irrelevant fields per park", "required_tool": "national-parks_findParks", "output_characteristics": "20+ parks, each with dozens of fields (description, images, activities, contacts, fees, etc.) — most are noise"}, {"step": 2, "description": "Scan the full park list and extract the specific park codes for 'Zion National Park' (zion) and 'Bryce Canyon National Park' (brca) — these codes are buried among many other park entries", "required_tool": null, "extraction_criteria": "Match fullName containing 'Zion' and 'Bryce', extract the parkCode field", "signal_to_remember": "zion_park_code and bryce_park_code"}, {"step": 3, "description": "Call national-parks_getParkDetails for Zion using the park code extracted in step 2; response is a very verbose object with many fields", "required_tool": "national-parks_getParkDetails", "dependency_on_step": 2, "output_characteristics": "Single park object with 30+ fields including images, activities, topics, contacts, fees, directions — most are noise"}, {"step": 4, "description": "Extract Zion's exact latitude and longitude from the verbose detail response — buried among many other fields", "required_tool": null, "extraction_criteria": "Find 'latitude' and 'longitude' fields in the park detail object", "signal_to_remember": "zion_latitude and zion_longitude"}, {"step": 5, "description": "Call national-parks_getParkDetails for Bryce Canyon using the park code from step 2; extract Bryce Canyon's latitude and longitude coordinates", "required_tool": "national-parks_getParkDetails", "dependency_on_step": 2, "output_characteristics": "Same verbose structure as Zion; must extract bryce_latitude and bryce_longitude"}, {"step": 6, "description": "Call national-parks_getAlerts using both park codes comma-separated; count the total number of active alerts across both parks", "required_tool": "national-parks_getAlerts", "dependency_on_step": 2, "output_characteristics": "Returns many alert objects each with full text, descriptions, URLs — must count them correctly"}, {"step": 7, "description": "Call google-maps_maps_distance_matrix using the Zion coordinates from step 4 and Bryce Canyon coordinates from step 5 (both sets of coordinates must be remembered across multiple noisy steps); extract driving distance and time from the response", "required_tool": "google-maps_maps_distance_matrix", "dependency_on_step": [4, 5], "uses_signal": "zion_latitude, zion_longitude, bryce_latitude, bryce_longitude"}]
EVALUATION_RULES: {"required_tools": ["national-parks_findParks", "national-parks_getParkDetails", "national-parks_getAlerts", "google-maps_maps_distance_matrix"], "forbidden_tools": [], "minimum_tool_calls": 6, "signal_extraction_step": 2, "signal_usage_step": 7, "noise_characteristics": "findParks returns a large list of all Utah parks with many irrelevant fields (description, images, topics, activities, contacts, entranceFees, etc.); getParkDetails returns a very verbose object with dozens of fields per park; getAlerts returns many alerts with full text, URLs, and metadata — agent must count only those matching the two specific parks."}
script_model_response:
I now have all the information you need. Here's your comprehensive trip planning summary:

---

## 🏞️ Utah National Parks Trip Comparison: Zion vs Bryce Canyon

### Park Information Summary

| Feature | Zion National Park | Bryce Canyon National Park |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Park Code** | `zion` | `brca` |
| **Coordinates** | 37.29839254, -113.0265138 | 37.58399144, -112.1826689 |
| **Location** | Springdale, UT | Bryce, UT |
| **Entrance Fee (vehicle)** | $35.00 | $35.00 |
| **Hours** | Open 24/7, year-round | Open 24/7, year-round |

---

### 🚗 (1) Driving Distance Between Parks

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| **Distance** | **137 km (approximately 85 miles)** |
| **Estimated Driving Time** | **1 hour 49 minutes** |

The route typically takes you through US-89, one of the most scenic highways in Utah!

---

### ⚠️ (2) Current Alerts Across Both Parks

| Park | Number of Alerts |
|------|------------------|
| **Zion National Park** | 5 alerts |
| **Bryce Canyon National Park** | 1 alert |
| **TOTAL ACTIVE ALERTS** | **6 alerts** |

#### Bryce Canyon Alerts (1):
1. **🚫 Wall Street side of Navajo Loop Closed** - The Wall St. side of Navajo Loop is expected to remain closed due to hazardous conditions.

#### Zion Alerts (5):
1. **🚛 Large Vehicle Restrictions** (effective June 7, 2026) - Vehicles exceeding size/weight limits won't be allowed on Zion-Mt. Carmel Highway
2. **⛰️ Angels Landing Permit Required** - Everyone hiking Angels Landing needs a permit
3. **🔥 Fire Restrictions in Effect** - Year-round fire restrictions throughout the park
4. **⚠️ Toxic Cyanobacteria Warning** - Don't submerge head in or filter drinking water from park waters
5. **🅿️ Parking Advisory** - Limited parking; use designated stalls only or park in Springdale

---

### Summary Box

| Your Question | Answer |
|---------------|--------|
| **(1) Driving Distance** | **137 km / ~85 miles** |
| **(2) Estimated Driving Time** | **1 hour 49 minutes** |
| **(3) Total Active Alerts** | **6 alerts** (5 at Zion + 1 at Bryce Canyon) |

---

**Pro Tip:** Since the drive between parks is less than 2 hours, you could easily visit both in a day trip, or better yet, spend a day or two at each to fully explore their unique features. Zion is famous for The Narrows and Angels Landing, while Bryce Canyon offers the world's largest concentration of hoodoos!

  "name": "Zion National Park",
  "code": "zion",
  "url": "https://www.nps.gov/zion/index.htm",
  "description": "Follow the paths where people have walked for thousands of years. Gaze up at massive sandstone cliffs of cream, pink, and red that soar into a brilliant blue sky. Experience the tall, stretching walls of a narrow slot canyon. Zion’s unique array of plants and animals will enchant you as you absorb the rich history of the past and enjoy the excitement of present-day adventures.",
  "designation": "National Park",
  "states": [
    "UT"
  ],
  "weatherInfo": "Zion is known for a wide range of weather conditions. Temperatures vary with changes in elevation and day/night temperatures may differ by over 30°F. In summer, temperatures in Zion National Park often exceed 100°F/38°C. Zion experiences monsoons from mid-July into September that result in an increased risk of flash floods. Always be aware of the threat of storms and lightning and be prepared for a wide range of weather conditions. Winters are generally mild.",
  "directionsInfo": "Zion National Park's main, south entrance and administrative offices are located near Springdale, Utah You may drive yourself on all open park roads except the Zion Canyon Scenic Drive. During most of the year, the Scenic Drive is accessed by shuttle bus only. Shuttles are free to ride. Board one in Springdale or at the Zion Canyon Visitor Center.",
  "directionsUrl": "http://www.nps.gov/zion/planyourvisit/directions.htm",
  "location": {
    "latitude": "37.29839254",

  "entranceFees": [
    {
      "title": "Entrance - Private Vehicle",
      "cost": "$35.00",
      "description": "Admits private, non-commercial vehicle (15 passenger capacity or less) and all occupants to Zion National Park. Valid for 1-7 days."
    },
    {
      "title": "Entrance - Motorcycle",
      "cost": "$30.00",
      "description": "Valid for 7 days. Admits up to 2 private, non-commercial motorcycles with up to 4 total passengers."
    },
    {
      "title": "Entrance - Snowmobile",
      "cost": "$20.00",
      "description": "Admits one individual with no car to Zion National Park. Typically used for bicyclists, hikers and pedestrians. Youth 15 and under are admitted free. Valid for 1-7 days."
    },
    {
      "title": "Entrance - Per Person",
      "cost": "$20.00",
      "description": "Admits one individual with no car to Zion National Park. Typically used for bicyclists, hikers and pedestrians. Youth 15 and under are admitted free. Valid for 1-7 days."
    },
    {
      "title": "Entrance - Non-commercial Groups",
      "cost": "$20.00",
      "description": "$20 per person. Non-commercial vehicles with a capacity of 16 or greater. Fees will not exceed the commercial fee for the same-sized vehicle. Youth 15 and under are free. Individuals or families with any valid Annual or Lifetime pass may use their pass for entry at the per person rate. Pass and photo ID must be present upon entry."
    },
    {
      "title": "Commercial Entrance - Van",
      "cost": "$70.00",
      "description": "A commercial tour is defined as consisting of one or more persons traveling on an itinerary that has been packaged, priced, or sold for leisure or recreational purposes by an organization that realizes financial gain through the provision of the service. The entry fees of commercial tours are based upon the total capacity of vehicles that can carry seven or more passengers regardless of the actual number of passengers."
    },
    {
      "title": "Commercial Entrance - Mini-bus",
      "cost": "$80.00",
      "description": "A commercial tour is defined as consisting of one or more persons traveling on an itinerary that has been packaged, priced, or sold for leisure or recreational purposes by an organization that realizes financial gain through the provision of the service. The entry fees of commercial tours are based upon the total capacity of vehicles that can carry seven or more passengers regardless of the actual number of passengers."
    },
    {
      "title": "Commercial Entrance - Motor Coach",
      "cost": "$190.00",
      "description": "A commercial tour is defined as consisting of one or more persons traveling on an itinerary that has been packaged, priced, or sold for leisure or recreational purposes by an organization that realizes financial gain through the provision of the service. The entry fees of commercial tours are based upon the total capacity of vehicles that can carry seven or more passengers regardless of the actual number of passengers."
    },
    {
      "title": "Commercial Entrance - Per Person",
      "cost": "$20.00",
      "description": "A commercial tour is defined as one or more persons traveling on an itinerary that has been packaged, priced, or sold for leisure or recreational purposes by an organization that realizes financial gain through the provision of the service. Vehicles with commercial tours of 1-6 people (not including guide) pay $35 per vehicle + $20 per person with a total fee not to exceed $70.\n\nPassengers can use valid weekly, annual, lifetime, or appreciation passes."
    },
    {
      "title": "Nonresident",
      "cost": "$100.00",
      "description": "Each non-US resident aged 16 and older visiting Zion National Park must pay a $100 nonresident fee (in addition to the standard entrance fee), unless admitted with an Annual or America the Beautiful Pass. This includes nonresidents entering as part of a commercial tour group, commercial use authorization (CUA) group, or concessioner tour group."
    }
  ],
  "entrancePasses": [

      "title": "Annual Entrance - Park",
      "cost": "$70.00",
      "description": "The Zion Annual Pass is valid only at Zion and may be purchased in person. This pass is valid for 12 months from purchase month. This pass admits the pass holder and passengers in a non-commercial vehicle. You can buy this entrance pass at all entrance stations."
    }

  "name": "Bryce Canyon National Park",
  "code": "brca",
  "url": "https://www.nps.gov/brca/index.htm",
  "description": "Hoodoos (irregular columns of rock) exist on every continent, but here is the largest concentration found anywhere on Earth. Situated along a high plateau at the top of the Grand Staircase, the park's high elevations include numerous life communities, fantastic dark skies, and geological wonders that defy description.",
  "designation": "National Park",
  "states": [
    "UT"
  ],
  "weatherInfo": "Due of its high elevation climate, weather at Bryce Canyon through autumn, winter, and spring can be highly variable. From October to May temperatures fall below freezing nearly every night. The park typically experiences its coldest and snowiest periods from December through February. Spring storms in March and April can still produce heavy snowfall that may impact travel in the region. Summer highs are typically in the 70s-80s F and afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August.",
  "directionsInfo": "From the North: Take I-15 south to UT-20 (exit 95). Travel east on UT-20 to US-89. Follow US-89 south to UT-12. Travel east on UT-12 to UT-63. Take UT-63 south to Bryce Canyon NP. From the South through Zion National Park: Take I-15 north to UT-9 (exit 16). Follow UT-9 east through Zion National Park to US-89. Travel north on US-89 to UT-12. Go east on UT-12 to UT-63. Take UT-63 south to Bryce Canyon NP. From the East Travel west on UT-12 to UT-63. Take UT-63 south to Bryce Canyon NP.",
  "directionsUrl": "http://www.nps.gov/brca/planyourvisit/directions.htm",
  "location": {
    "latitude": "37.58399144",
    "longitude": "-112.1826689",
    "address": {
      "line1": "Highway 63",
      "line2": "Bryce Canyon National Park",

  "images": [

      "url": "https://www.nps.gov/common/uploads/structured_data/61F08520-E14F-18F2-BF5F3D89482631BD.jpg",
      "title": "Bryce Amphitheater from Inspiration Point",
      "altText": "A red rock landscape and plateau forest glows with the morning sun",
      "caption": "Viewpoints of the iconic Bryce Amphitheater are located along the first 3 miles of the park road and are a popular destination at sunrise.",
      "credit": "NPS Photo / Peter Densmore"
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.nps.gov/common/uploads/structured_data/600067EF-E1B2-18B1-6120394EC23521DB.jpg",
      "title": "Winter sunrise at Sunset Point",
      "altText": "Snow blankets a red rock landscape of tall rock spires beneath an early morning sky",
      "caption": "Here at 8,000' (2438 m) winter comes early and stays late. Snow transforms the landscape and requires seasonal closures in some areas and trails.",
      "credit": "NPS Photo / Keith Moore"
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.nps.gov/common/uploads/structured_data/613FFA94-B2F5-FBBF-090B96462B7CAB18.jpg",
      "title": "Milky Way over the Hoodoos",
      "altText": "The center of the Milky Way galaxy is seen rising above a horizon of forest and red rock spires",
      "caption": "Bryce Canyon's clean air and dark night skies are some of its most precious and vulnerable resources. Full moon nights also provide a unique experience of seeing the park after dark.",
      "credit": "Derek Demeter"
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.nps.gov/common/uploads/structured_data/60400EC2-B3DA-DAD5-18BF97EA3403B692.jpg",
      "title": "Queen's Garden Trail",
      "altText": "A lone white rock tower stands surrounded by red rock walls and forest along a trail",
      "caption": "More strenuous trails below the rim provide up-close views of the hoodoo rock spires, while easier walks along the rim give perspective from above.",
      "credit": "NPS Photo / Peter Densmore"
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.nps.gov/common/uploads/structured_data/609317A5-D0B1-4B67-C2413B5852192FB6.jpg",
      "title": "Bristlecone Loop trail near Yovimpa Point",
      "altText": "Red and white rock cliffs lightly dusted with snow with a forest atop them and a long view beyond",
      "caption": "The park's highest elevations at its southern end and backcountry areas provide views of over 100 miles (161 km) atop high steep cliffs.",
      "credit": "NPS Photo / Peter Densmore"
    }
  ]
